question_title: Will the community prediction be higher than 79.00% on 2026-03-12 for the Metaculus question "Will the Community beat Nathan Young in the Metaculus Cup Spring 2026?"?
summary: Meta-prediction with CP exactly at the 79% threshold. Strictly greater than required, so status quo = NO. Community predictions historically outperform individuals (rank ~4th site-wide), providing mild upward drift pressure. But Nathan Young is a skilled professional, and no CP trajectory data is available to confirm direction. Net: near coin-flip with slight lean toward YES based on crowd advantage fundamentals.

factors.description: CP at exactly the threshold (79.00%) — strict inequality means staying put resolves NO, creating slight asymmetric bias against YES
Community Prediction ranks ~4th on all of Metaculus, consistently outperforms most individual forecasters in tournaments — fundamental support for upward CP drift
Nathan Young is a skilled professional forecaster (Swift Centre, former Head of Forecasting at Zeitgeist, Metaculus/Manifold moderator) — reduces expected crowd advantage vs a random individual
Tournament ongoing with questions still resolving — information flow should clarify who's winning, and if community is ahead (as 79% CP suggests), confidence should rise
No CP trajectory data available (API 403) — cannot assess whether CP was trending up, down, or stable. Critical information gap for meta-prediction.
Incentive structure ($2,500 bonus if community wins) could attract more participation and improve community prediction quality

reasoning:
My forecast: **52% probability** that the CP will be above 79.00% on March 12.

This is essentially a near-coin-flip with a very slight lean toward YES. The reasoning: the community prediction has a strong structural track record (ranks ~4th on all of Metaculus), which provides mild upward drift pressure on the CP. However, the CP is at *exactly* the threshold, strict inequality is required (staying at 79% = NO), and Nathan Young is an experienced professional forecaster — not a trivial benchmark. The critical missing piece is CP trajectory data (403 error), which would normally be the dominant input for meta-prediction questions.
